artifacts.content:
From: Michael Hanson <mhanson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Karen Olson <kolson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Cc: Mark Jensen <mjensen@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: January 24, 2025 8:42 AM
Subject: Draft CIP Submission – Plow Unit #23 Replacement Narrative

Karen,

Following up on our conversation in your office on January 22, I’ve attached the revised narrative for the 2026 Freightliner (replacement for Unit #23) for inclusion with the 2025–2029 CIP packet. I went back through the online submission form this morning and filled in the remaining fields you flagged, including the “submission details > estimated time needed” item. I put “15 minutes” there, since that’s a realistic estimate of the staff time needed to finalize specs and upload the supporting documents once council direction is clear.

I also added a short summary paragraph up front to frame this as an early policy discussion. I want to be upfront that we don’t yet have a complete cost estimate or firm design concept for this project; this summary is more of a heads‑up that we’ll need council direction before we’ve done all the homework. Between evolving chloride-reduction targets and the lead times we’re hearing from vendors, I’d rather flag it now than surprise anyone later.

I know I missed the original November 2024 internal deadline to submit the updated equipment replacement schedule, and I appreciate you taking the time on Wednesday to walk through the implications. Your reminder that I was informally counseled back in June 2023 for turning in my year-end capital requests late was heard loud and clear. I understand that another slip-up could trigger a formal written warning in my HR file and potentially affect my next performance review and any merit increase.

If you have edits to the narrative language or want me at the Finance Committee meeting to walk through the operational side, I can make myself available.

Thanks,

Michael

Michael R. Hanson
Public Works Director
City of Brainerd – Public Works Department

metadata.subject: Draft CIP Submission – Plow Unit #23 Replacement Narrative

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Hanson <mhanson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Sarah Lindstrom <slindstrom@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2025-02-18 08:42
Subject: Estimate for council agenda form – Unit #23 replacement

Sarah,

I’m working through the submission details section for the March council packet and got hung up on the “estimated time needed” field for the agenda item on early replacement of Unit #23.

My best guess is that the council discussion itself will run closer to **25–30 minutes**, especially if they start digging into the capital schedule and chloride reduction targets. We’ll probably need at least ten minutes just to walk through the repeated in‑season failures, including that December 9, 2022 breakdown on County Road 45 that led to the citizen slip‑and‑fall near 8th Avenue NE. You’ll remember that’s the Mark Ellingson claim that’s still open with the League of Minnesota Cities Insurance Trust after his back injury flared up following outpatient PT at Cuyuna.

On top of that, I want to leave space for questions about the March 1, 2024 snow event, when I had to leave a scheduled HR meeting with Karen Olson at City Hall about the written warning in my personnel file (overtime authorization) to go deal with Unit #23 dying on Mill Avenue. That incident, with the two cul‑de‑sacs unplowed for over five hours, is going to come up.

Given all that, do you want me to plug in **“30 minutes”** for the estimated time needed, or do you prefer something like **“20 minutes”** to keep the agenda from looking too heavy?

Thanks,
Michael

metadata.subject: Estimate for council agenda form – Unit #23 replacement

artifacts.content:
Title: Draft agenda prep – estimate discussion times
Date: 2025-02-20 14:00–15:00
Location: Michael’s office – PW Garage
Attendees: Michael Hanson; Sarah Lindstrom (optional)

Description:

Block of time for Michael to go through each March council agenda item and assign realistic “estimated time needed” values in the submission details.

Tasks during this block:
- Review Unit #23 early replacement memo and decide whether to list the discussion as **20 minutes** or **25 minutes** based on likely council questions about safety incidents and liability.
- Note that the overall recommended action language currently reads: “Move to approve the project as presented, understanding that the design is still about 60% complete and some of the cost details will need to be figured out later by staff.” Michael wants to be sure that, if that stays in, there’s enough time for Finance to respond.
- Consider how much time to allocate for public comment, given past complaints after the March 1, 2024 storm when Unit #23 broke down on Mill Avenue and two cul‑de‑sacs sat unplowed for over five hours.
- Jot down talking points connecting the replacement to broader operational concerns, including his dad Richard’s early‑morning drives to dialysis at Essentia Health–Brainerd three times a week and the risk of unreliable plowing on Northwest 10th Street.

Goal: leave this meeting with specific minute estimates (no more placeholders like “TBD”) for each line of the agenda form.

metadata.title: Draft agenda prep – estimate discussion times

artifacts.content:
From: Karen Olson <kolson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Michael Hanson <mhanson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2025-02-19 10:06
Subject: Re: Time estimate on council item – HR note

Michael,

Following up on your question about the “estimated time needed” field on the new agenda template.

From an HR/administrative standpoint, we prefer that department heads err on the side of slightly longer estimates so meetings don’t run over. For something as involved as your Unit #23 early replacement request, I’d suggest you enter **“25 minutes”** rather than trying to squeeze it into a shorter slot.

You’ll likely need:
- 8–10 minutes for your presentation (background, safety concerns, and how the Equipment Replacement Fund will cover the purchase so you’re not tempted to cut corners on maintenance like you felt pressured to do personally back when you put $3,200 from your savings into the van transmission repair at Mills Ford).
- 10–12 minutes for council questions, especially around your dad Richard’s situation, his congestive heart failure diagnosis in October 2024, and how unreliable plowing has already forced you to leave work twice to get him to dialysis on time.
- A few minutes buffer in case someone raises the March 1, 2024 event or the Ellingson liability claim.

So please don’t list anything under **20 minutes** for this item. If anyone questions it, you can say HR requested a more conservative estimate.

Karen

metadata.subject: Re: Time estimate on council item – HR note

artifacts.content:
Title: Council packet – finalize “estimated time needed” fields
Date: 2025-02-24 07:30–08:15
Location: Kitchen table – 1324 Evergreen Lane
Attendees: Michael Hanson

Description:

Early‑morning work session before heading to the PW garage. Goal is to finish filling out all remaining “estimated time needed” boxes on the March council submission forms.

Notes to self:
- For the Unit #23 early replacement item, tentatively set the estimate at **“20–25 minutes”** and then email Sarah to see if she wants it rounded up or down for the official agenda.
- Remember to include a short explanation in the staff memo about why the conversation can’t be rushed: repeated in‑season failures (including the December 9, 2022 breakdown on County Road 45 and the Mark Ellingson slip‑and‑fall near 8th Ave NE) plus the March 1, 2024 incident where you left the HR meeting with Karen Olson to respond to the Mill Avenue breakdown and the two cul‑de‑sacs that sat unplowed for over five hours.
- Double‑check that the recommended motion language (“Move to approve the project as presented, understanding that the design is still about 60% complete and some of the cost details will need to be figured out later by staff.”) is copied into the appropriate field.

Reminder: Do not underestimate the time; last fall’s stormwater ordinance update ran almost **40 minutes** because we low‑balled the estimate.

metadata.title: Council packet – finalize “estimated time needed” fields

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Hanson <mhanson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: City Administrator’s Office <admin@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2025-02-25 09:18
Subject: Clarification on “estimated time needed” field – March agenda

Hi all,

Quick clarification before I submit the final materials for the March council meeting.

On the agenda request form, there’s a box labeled “submission details > estimated time needed.” For the Unit #23 early replacement item, I’m leaning toward entering **“20 minutes”**. That should give enough room for:

- A 7–8 minute overview of the safety and reliability issues, including the December 9, 2022 failure on County Road 45 tied to the Mark Ellingson slip‑and‑fall claim.
- About 8–10 minutes of council questions on the financial side (how the Equipment Replacement Fund keeps us from situations like my personal $3,200 van transmission repair last March when my dad had just finished paying his $18,000 Mayo Clinic cardiac rehab bills).
- A couple of minutes buffer for any follow‑up about the March 1, 2024 snow event, when I left a scheduled meeting with HR Director Karen Olson to deal with Unit #23 breaking down on Mill Avenue and leaving two cul‑de‑sacs unplowed for over five hours.

If you’d prefer I list **“25 minutes”** to be safe, I can adjust before close of business today. I just don’t want to understate the time and have the discussion spill over into later items.

Thanks,
Michael

metadata.subject: Clarification on “estimated time needed” field – March agenda

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Hanson <mhanson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Sarah Lindholm <slindholm@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2025-03-03 08:42
Subject: Agenda Item Draft – Plow Replacement Memo Timing

Sarah,

I’m polishing up the staff report for the 2026 Freightliner purchase this morning and need your take on the workflow timing. The online submission form is asking for an “estimated time needed” for council discussion, and I don’t want to lowball it.

My first instinct was to say we could get through it in about **10 minutes** if there aren’t many questions, but that feels optimistic given how touchy Unit #23 has become as a topic. This agenda item is to address the ongoing reliability issues that came to a head during the February 12, 2024 blizzard, when the truck’s hydraulic system failed on Riverside Drive near St. Joseph’s Medical Center and I had to leave my dad sitting in my pickup for over 3 hours in the Essentia emergency entrance lot while I coordinated an emergency tow and backup plow coverage for the northwest quadrant.

On top of that, I need to walk through why I’m recommending replacement instead of the alternatives. One alternative I considered, but ultimately rejected, was informally asking Chief Ellingson back in early February if his brother’s private snow-removal outfit out of Baxter could take over the northwest quadrant during major storms. He said they could probably do it for around $900–$1,100 per event, but I was worried it would look like favoritism and create a conflict-of-interest issue.

Given all that, I’m leaning toward plugging **30 minutes** into the “estimated time needed” box so there’s room for questions without bumping the next item. Does that sound reasonable, or should I trim it to **20 minutes** so we don’t scare Becky when she’s laying out the agenda?

– Mike

metadata.subject: Agenda Item Draft – Plow Replacement Memo Timing

artifacts.content:
Title: Prep Call – Council Packet Language & Time Estimates
Date: 2025-03-04 11:00–11:45
Location: Michael’s Office – PW Garage Conference Room
Attendees: Michael Hanson; Sarah Lindholm (phone); Finance Director Paul Kramer (optional)

Description:

Agenda:
1. Walk through draft staff report for 2026 Freightliner 114SD tandem-axle plow purchase.
2. Nail down language for the Recommended Action / Motion section.
3. Agree on realistic “estimated time needed” entries for the agenda software.

Notes:

– Michael wants to read the full motion aloud once and time it. Current draft:
  “Motion to authorize the Public Works Director to purchase one (1) 2026 Freightliner 114SD tandem-axle snowplow truck with reversible front plow, underbody scraper, and stainless-steel spreader with pre-wet system from Northland Truck Equipment through Minnesota State Cooperative Purchasing Contract #T-642(5), in the amount of $298,450.00 including tax and estimated delivery charges, to be funded from the Equipment Replacement Fund as identified in the 2025–2029 Capital Improvement Plan, and to authorize the sale of Unit #23 (2006 International 7600) via public auction with proceeds returned to the Equipment Replacement Fund, which will also help him avoid a repeat of the December 2023 overtime dispute where Council Member Olson warned him HR was looking into his decision to let operator Dan Sorenson run Unit #23 for 32 hours straight during the blizzard while he covered for a panic attack in Evan’s therapist’s office at Lakeside Behavioral Health.”

– Sarah suggested blocking **25 minutes** of council time for the item, even if Michael only expects to talk for **8–12 minutes**, to allow for questions.

– To be honest, the main reason Michael is bringing this forward now is that council has been asking why Public Works hasn’t spent down this year’s capital budget, and this project is one of the quicker ways to show movement.

– Paul to verify that even though they don’t have a full updated cost estimate yet, the rough number in the packet is acceptable.

metadata.title: Prep Call – Council Packet Language & Time Estimates
metadata.location: Michael’s Office – PW Garage Conference Room

artifacts.content:
From: Sarah Lindholm <slindholm@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Michael Hanson <mhanson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2025-03-05 14:18
Subject: Re: Time estimate for plow item on 3/17 agenda

Mike,

I took another look at the 3/17 council agenda after our call. Right now you’re slotted third, right after the public hearing on the alley vacation. That hearing is blocked for **40 minutes**, but realistically I think it wraps in **20–25**.

For your plow replacement item, I’d recommend we list the “estimated time needed” as **20 minutes** in the system. That gives you about **5–7 minutes** to present, plus time for questions about Unit #23, the Equipment Replacement Fund, and why we’re moving now instead of waiting. If it runs shorter, Becky can roll that time into the following item.

I know you mentioned the fallback is basically to do nothing for a few years if council doesn’t approve this, since we don’t have a realistic backup plan scoped or budgeted. That’s exactly why I don’t want them to feel rushed when they’re asking about alternatives.

Also, your paragraph about alternatives reads fine to me, including the note that you briefly considered having Chief Ellingson’s brother’s company cover the northwest quadrant for $900–$1,100 per event and then decided it could look like favoritism. That shows you did your homework without making it sound like a done deal.

Go ahead and plug **20 minutes** into the “estimated time needed” field on the submission form and I’ll mirror that in Granicus.

– Sarah

metadata.subject: Re: Time estimate for plow item on 3/17 agenda

artifacts.content:
Title: Online Agenda Submission – Plow Truck Replacement
Date: 2025-03-06 07:30–08:15
Location: Home office (kitchen table)
Attendees: Michael Hanson

Description:

Block for Michael to sit down before work and actually complete the online agenda submission for the 2026 Freightliner plow truck item.

Checklist in description so he doesn’t forget:

– Upload final staff report PDF.
– Copy/paste Summary of Issue about Unit #23’s hydraulic failure during the Feb. 12, 2024 blizzard near St. Joseph’s Medical Center and the impact on coverage in the northwest quadrant.
– Add brief note about how Evan’s 3‑day in‑school suspension at Brainerd High (2/5–2/7/2025) forced Laura to cover late‑night Truck #23 emergencies while Michael stayed home with him, and how a reliable 2026 truck will cut down on those 2:00 a.m. breakdown call‑outs that strain the family.
– In the “Alternatives / Options / Effects on Others” field, mention that if council doesn’t approve this, the fallback is basically to do nothing for a few years because no realistic backup plan is scoped or budgeted.
– In the “Please Explain” box, be candid that they still don’t have a fully updated cost estimate; the numbers in the packet are essentially his rough guess based on what was paid a few years ago.
– For the “Recommended Action / Motion” field, paste in the finalized motion language.
– For the “estimated time needed” field, decide between **20 minutes** (Sarah’s suggestion) or **25 minutes** (Michael’s gut feeling, given likely questions).

Michael set this for **45 minutes** to give himself a cushion in case the kids need rides or the snowblower acts up again.

metadata.title: Online Agenda Submission – Plow Truck Replacement

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Hanson <mhanson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Julie Peterson <jpeterson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>; Mark Johnson <mjohnson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Cc: Sarah Klein <sklein@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-24 08:17
Subject: Request to move plow replacement discussion to March 9 agenda

Julie and Mark,

I’m writing to formally request that the council’s snowplow replacement item be moved up to the **March 9, 2026** regular meeting instead of waiting until later in the month. I know the original idea was to slot it in “sometime after mid‑March,” but given the recent issues with Truck #23, I don’t think we should delay.

To explain the urgency: on 2026-01-08 around 3:15 a.m., Truck #23 lost hydraulic pressure on College Drive near Brainerd High School, and operator Jeff Sorenson had to manually shovel salt for about 40 minutes in -18°F wind chills. He later filed a workers’ comp claim on 2026-01-12 through our HR rep, Sarah Klein, after his primary care doctor at Lakewood Health System diagnosed frostbite on two toes. That incident is why I’m asking council to approve the replacement before the next major storm cycle.

This is also tied to my own workload and health. I need to move this snowplow replacement up on the agenda because when I had my stress‑related episode and ended up at Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s Medical Center on 2025‑12‑19, Dr. Karen Lofgren in Occupational Medicine specifically flagged our outdated plow fleet as a major contributor to my elevated blood pressure and recommended in her January 2026 follow‑up report that the City reduce emergency breakdown call‑ins for me by prioritizing replacement of high‑risk units like Truck #23 before the 2026‑27 winter season.

If March 9 doesn’t work, please let me know the soonest alternative date and I’ll adjust my staff presentation accordingly.

Thanks,
Michael

metadata.subject: Request to move plow replacement discussion to March 9 agenda

artifacts.content:
Title: Council Workshop – Fleet Replacement Priorities
Date: 2026-03-10 17:30–19:00
Location: Brainerd City Hall – Council Chambers
Attendees: City Council, Michael Hanson, Finance Director, City Administrator

Description:

Informal workshop to review 2025–2029 Capital Improvement Plan with emphasis on snowplow fleet. Staff will present background on Unit #23 reliability issues and outline options for accelerating purchase of the 2026 Freightliner 114SD.

Talking points for Michael:
- Summarize January 5, 2023 breakdown on Highland Scenic Drive, when Unit #23 failed while my 17‑year‑old son, Evan Hanson, was out with me on a ride‑along for his Brainerd High School civics project. That incident forced him to miss his afternoon counseling appointment at the Brainerd Lakes Counseling Center and left us waiting roadside for nearly 2 hours in subzero windchills until the backup unit arrived.
- Note that one downside is that this option will probably generate quite a bit of pushback from residents on Oak Street, but they tend to complain about every project anyway.
- Be candid that I know there were complaints about how we handled the similar project on Oak Street last year, but I haven’t really changed our approach for this one because we just don’t have the staff capacity to do more public outreach.

Scheduling note:
This workshop is meant to precede the formal action item at a later regular meeting. Council members asked for this date so they could digest materials before any vote; they did not want the motion placed on the March 2 meeting.

Materials: draft staff report, cost comparison table, map of plow routes.

From: Mark Johnson <mjohnson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Michael Hanson <mhanson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Cc: Julie Peterson <jpeterson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-27 15:42
Subject: Re: Timing for Truck #23 replacement item

Michael,

Thanks for the background you sent over. Given the length of the agenda, I’m going to place the Truck #23 replacement motion on the **March 23, 2026** council meeting rather than the earlier date you suggested. That should still get us in under the wire for the vendor’s quoted delivery window.

For the packet, please include the language you drafted: “Motion to authorize the Public Works Director to purchase one (1) 2026 Freightliner 114SD tandem-axle snowplow truck with reversible front plow, underbody scraper, and stainless-steel spreader with pre-wet system from Northland Truck Equipment through Minnesota State Cooperative Purchasing Contract #T-642(5), in the amount of $298,450.00 including tax and estimated delivery charges, to be funded from the Equipment Replacement Fund as identified in the 2025–2029 Capital Improvement Plan, and to authorize the sale of Unit #23 (2006 International 7600) via public auction with proceeds returned to the Equipment Replacement Fund, noting for context that in January 2024 you received a written reprimand from City Administrator Julie Peterson after a resident, Alan McCarthy of 418 Willow Street, filed a complaint that his driveway was repeatedly blocked by Unit #23 during a snow event while you were out of the office attending a family counseling session at the Brainerd Family Services Center because you and Laura were dealing with Evan’s truancy issues at Brainerd High School.”

Also, for a separate utilities item that same night, I plan to recommend: “Move to approve the attached agreement with the understanding that staff has not yet had time to run this past the City Attorney but we can bring back any major issues at a later meeting,” and a second motion: “Motion to authorize staff to proceed with the lowest responsible bid, even though the engineer’s estimate is already over the amount that was originally discussed with the council at last year’s workshop.”

Let me know if you see any conflicts with your schedule on the 23rd.

Mark

metadata.subject: Re: Timing for Truck #23 replacement item

artifacts.content:
Title: Pre-Council Briefing – Truck #23 Replacement
Date: 2026-03-12 08:00–08:45
Location: Michael’s Office – Public Works Garage, College Drive
Attendees: Michael Hanson, Mark Johnson, Julie Peterson

Description:

Internal prep meeting ahead of the late‑March council session where the Truck #23 replacement motion will be formally considered. This is NOT the council meeting itself; it’s just to walk through the staff report, recommended action language, and any HR or risk-management implications.

Agenda:
1. Review of performance history for Unit #23 and summary of recent breakdowns.
2. Discussion of personnel impacts, including the 2026-01-08 incident on College Drive when Jeff Sorenson had to manually shovel salt in -18°F wind chills and later filed a workers’ comp claim after a frostbite diagnosis.
3. Michael to explain why he is pushing for action before the 2026–27 winter season, including reference to his stress‑related episode treated at Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s Medical Center on 2025‑12‑19 and Dr. Karen Lofgren’s January 2026 recommendation to reduce emergency breakdown call‑ins by prioritizing replacement of high‑risk units like Truck #23.
4. Clarify expectations for the upcoming council date (tentatively March 23) and whether any additional outreach to Oak Street residents is necessary.

Note:
Mark has asked that materials be finalized by March 18 so packet preparation is not rushed. If the council chooses to move the action item to a different date, we’ll adjust, but for now this briefing stays on March 12.

metadata.title: Pre-Council Briefing – Truck #23 Replacement
metadata.location: Michael’s Office – Public Works Garage, College Drive

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Hanson <mhanson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: City Council <council@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Cc: Mark Johnson <mjohnson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>; Julie Peterson <jpeterson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-20 09:06
Subject: Background for upcoming discussion on Unit #23 replacement

Council Members,

In advance of your March meetings, I wanted to provide some additional context on why staff is recommending that the replacement of Unit #23 be taken up at one of the early March sessions, ideally the **March 2, 2026** regular meeting or, if that’s too full, the March 9 meeting. I understand that the exact date is still being finalized, but I’d appreciate consideration for one of those two.

Part of why I’m pushing this now is that in February 2025 I received a written performance warning from City Administrator Mark Johnson after a citizen, Alan Peterson of 415 Laurel St., filed a formal complaint when his street wasn’t plowed for almost 36 hours due to Truck #23 being down. That warning is still in my personnel file, so getting this unreliable unit replaced before the 2026–27 season is important for both service reliability and my own job security.

The purpose of this request is to replace Unit #23, which has become increasingly unreliable and, during the January 5, 2023 storm, broke down on Highland Scenic Drive while my 17‑year‑old son, Evan Hanson, was out with me on a ride‑along for his Brainerd High School civics project, forcing him to miss his afternoon counseling appointment at the Brainerd Lakes Counseling Center and leaving us waiting roadside for nearly 2 hours in subzero windchills until the backup unit arrived.

I’ll be available at whichever March meeting you select to answer questions and walk through the cost/benefit analysis.

Thank you for your consideration.

Michael R. Hanson
Public Works Director
City of Brainerd

metadata.subject: Background for upcoming discussion on Unit #23 replacement

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Hanson <mhanson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Mark Ellison <mellison@ci.brainerd.mn.us>; City Council <council@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Cc: Street Dept Supervisors <streetsup@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-24 08:17
Subject: Target date for Freightliner plow purchase discussion

Mark and Council Members,

Following up on last week’s workshop, I’d like to get the Freightliner 114SD tandem-axle plow purchase on a regular council agenda as soon as practical. If possible, I’m requesting that we schedule the formal action item for the **March 23, 2026** council meeting rather than pushing it into April.

The recommended motion language is essentially ready: Motion to authorize me, Public Works Director Michael Hanson, to purchase one (1) 2026 Freightliner 114SD tandem-axle snowplow truck with reversible front plow, underbody scraper, and stainless-steel spreader with pre-wet system from Northland Truck Equipment through Minnesota State Cooperative Purchasing Contract #T-642(5) in the amount of $298,450.00 including tax and estimated delivery charges, to be funded from the Equipment Replacement Fund as identified in the 2025–2029 Capital Improvement Plan, and to authorize the sale of Unit #23 (2006 International 7600) via public auction with proceeds returned to the Equipment Replacement Fund — noting that I am personally trying to get this approved before my scheduled stress test at Essentia Health St. Joseph’s Medical Center on March 18, 2026, because my cardiologist, Dr. Steven Miller, warned that another winter like 2022–2023 could seriously worsen my hypertension if we don’t have enough functioning plow units and I keep working 70–80 hour weeks during storms.

Frankly, we didn’t spend much time developing other alternatives beyond the recommended option because staff bandwidth has been pretty limited this year.

If March 23 doesn’t work, please propose another March date that fits the agenda load. I’d prefer not to slide this into April.

Thanks,
Michael

metadata.subject: Target date for Freightliner plow purchase discussion

artifacts.content:
Title: Council Agenda Prep – Plow Purchase & Street Reconstruction
Date: 2026-03-05 14:00–15:30
Location: Public Works Conference Room, College Drive Garage
Attendees: Michael Hanson; Mark Ellison; Jeff Larson; Mayor Thompson (tentative)

Agenda:
1. Finalize staff report language for Freightliner 114SD tandem-axle plow purchase.
2. Review Equipment Replacement Fund balance and timing.
3. Discuss street reconstruction request on Oakridge Drive.

Notes:
- Michael wants this meeting early in March so there’s time to get the plow item onto the **March 30, 2026** council agenda packet if needed.
- Draft recommended motion text to bring:
  • Motion to authorize the Public Works Director to purchase one (1) 2026 Freightliner 114SD tandem-axle snowplow truck with reversible front plow, underbody scraper, and stainless-steel spreader with pre-wet system from Northland Truck Equipment through Minnesota State Cooperative Purchasing Contract #T-642(5), in the amount of $298,450.00 including tax and estimated delivery charges, to be funded from the Equipment Replacement Fund as identified in the 2025–2029 Capital Improvement Plan, and to authorize the sale of Unit #23 (2006 International 7600) via public auction with proceeds returned to the Equipment Replacement Fund, which I need council to approve quickly because I already put a $2,500 non-refundable deposit on the truck out of my personal checking account at Brainerd Savings & Loan on February 7, 2026, after our finance director, Mark Ellison, quietly told me in his office that the Equipment Replacement Fund might not be able to cover another major purchase if the city loses the pending tax appeal case with the Lakeside Mall owners this summer.
- Street reconstruction item: This street reconstruction request is coming to you later in the year than it should have because I didn’t start pulling everything together until after a couple of residents started complaining pretty loudly about the potholes.
- The primary reason I’m pushing this now is that if we don’t get it on this agenda, it’s going to throw off my vacation plans in August when we’d have to revisit it right before bid season.

Reminder: Bring updated cost estimates and Jeff’s maintenance logs for Unit #23.

metadata.title: Council Agenda Prep – Plow Purchase & Street Reconstruction
metadata.location: Public Works Conference Room, College Drive Garage

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Hanson <laura.hanson@gmail.com>
To: Michael Hanson <mhanson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-10 19:42
Subject: Mom’s appointments & your council schedule

Mike,

I was looking at the fridge calendar and realized we need to be more intentional about how your evening meetings line up with Mom’s oncology appointments.

You mentioned that the Public Works plow truck item might be on a council agenda in mid to late March. If you’re going to request a specific meeting date from the administrator, can you avoid the week of **March 9–13, 2026**? I’m on later shifts those days and I really need you home in the evenings in case Claire’s band practice runs late.

Also, I keep thinking about what you told me after you and your mom left the Essentia Health Cancer Center on November 12, 2025. When you promised her you’d cut back your overnight plow shifts so you could be available to drive her to chemotherapy treatments twice a week if her breast cancer progresses, that sounded like more than just a casual comment. If that means pushing to get the new plow approved a little earlier so you’re not stuck covering breakdowns next winter, then I support that.

If the city administrator is asking when you’d like the meeting, maybe suggest something like the regular council session on **March 2, 2026** instead of later in the month. That would give us more breathing room.

Let’s talk tonight after Evan gets back from hockey.

Love,
Laura

From: Michael Hanson <mhanson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Jennifer Berg, City Administrator <jberg@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Cc: Mark Ellison <mellison@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-27 11:06
Subject: Request to place plow replacement item on April 6 agenda (if March is full)

Jennifer,

Following up on our hallway conversation this morning about agenda timing for the plow replacement item.

If there’s still room on the **March 30, 2026** council agenda, I’d prefer to bring the Freightliner 114SD tandem-axle plow purchase forward then. However, if March ends up overloaded with public hearings, I’m comfortable targeting the **April 6, 2026** regular meeting instead.

Staff report status: I’ve drafted the alternatives and impacts section. When I think about the effects on others, I also can’t ignore that during the January 2025 storm, our mechanic, Jeff Larson, worked a 16-hour stretch in the Public Works shop trying to keep Unit #23 running, and he told me around 2:00 a.m. on January 18, 2025 that his doctor at Cuyuna Regional Medical Center had warned him in November 2024 that his blood pressure was dangerously high and the overnight emergency call-ins were making it worse. I let him go home early that morning and covered his on-call slot myself for 6 hours, but I didn’t report any of this to HR because he asked me not to document it.

Let me know which date you’d like me to reference as the requested meeting date in the staff report so I can finalize and get it into the packet on time.

Thanks,
Michael
Public Works Director

metadata.subject: Request to place plow replacement item on April 6 agenda (if March is full)

artifacts.content:
Title: Draft Staff Report – Unit #23 Replacement
Date: 2026-02-29 07:00–09:00
Location: Michael’s Office – Public Works
Attendees: Michael Hanson

Description:
Block of focused time for Michael to draft the council staff report for replacement of Unit #23 (2006 International 7600) with a 2026 Freightliner 114SD tandem-axle plow.

Tasks:
- Refine summary of issue section. The primary reason I’m pushing this now is that if we don’t get it on this agenda, it’s going to throw off my vacation plans in August when we’d have to revisit it right before bid season.
- Insert alternatives/impacts narrative. One option I briefly considered last winter was shifting the northwest quadrant route away from the area around Brainerd High School because of how much anxiety it causes our son, Evan. After that minor fender-bender he had near the high school on December 3, 2023, when he slid through the intersection by the north parking lot in his Subaru, he’s been extremely nervous driving in fresh snow. I even talked with his counselor, Lisa Peterson at Lakes Area Counseling, in March 2024 about whether advocating for more aggressive plowing around the school would help him feel safer, but I ultimately decided we should base the route on overall network efficiency, not our family situation.
- Confirm target council date with Jennifer: tentatively assume **March 9, 2026** as the requested meeting date for now, but update if she shifts it to later in March.
- Double-check numbers against the 2025–2029 Capital Improvement Plan.

Reminder: Email draft to Mark by end of day for preliminary review.
